lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — The biosimilar challenge
Which brings us, naturally, to biosimilars.
A biosimilar is a follow-on biologic, made by a different manufacturer, intended to be clinically equivalent to an originator biologic whose patents have expired.
The biosimilar concept is well-established for small molecules — a generic aspirin is, atom-for-atom, identical to brand-name aspirin.
But for biologics, identity is impossible.

The protein sequence can be identical, but the glycoform, the disulfide bond pattern, the higher-order structure, the impurity profile, the host cell residuals — these depend on the manufacturing process, and no two processes produce exactly the same product.
Biosimilars are not generics.
They are similar, by regulatory definition, but not identical.
For a non-glycoengineered antibody like rituximab, the biosimilar challenge is significant but tractable.
The originator's glycoform profile is well-characterized, and a biosimilar developer aims to produce an antibody whose glycoform falls within a defined window of the originator.
Some variation is acceptable; large deviations are not.
Multiple rituximab biosimilars have been approved in the US, Europe, and elsewhere, and clinical experience has been broadly equivalent.
For a glycoengineered antibody like obinutuzumab or mogamulizumab, the biosimilar challenge is much harder.
The glycoform is the mechanism.
A biosimilar that produces 70% afucose where the originator produces 95% afucose is, functionally, a substantially less potent drug.
Matching to within a few percent on a defined and unusual glycoform requires using essentially the same engineering platform — and platforms are protected by patent and trade secret.
You can't just copy the technology; you have to develop your own afucose engineering, achieve comparable glycoform homogeneity, and demonstrate clinical equivalence.
This is a real barrier.
As of the mid-2020s, biosimilars of mogamulizumab and obinutuzumab have been slower to emerge than biosimilars of conventional antibodies.
The technical and regulatory bar is higher.
The platforms required are more specialized.
The investment required to develop a glycoengineered biosimilar is larger.
The clinical analogy I want you to hold is: matching innovator glycoforms is hard — subtle batch-to-batch differences affect efficacy.
A 5% drift in afucose content can be a meaningful potency change.
A shift in the galactose distribution can change CDC behavior.

Even for the originator company, maintaining glycoform consistency across years of manufacturing — through process changes, facility transfers, scale changes — is a constant analytical and engineering effort.
For a biosimilar manufacturer trying to match a moving target with a different process, it's even harder.
There's a regulatory dimension here too.
The FDA, EMA, and other regulators have developed specific guidance for biosimilar development that explicitly addresses glycoform characterization.

Sponsors are expected to demonstrate that their biosimilar matches the originator on a defined panel of glycan-related quality attributes, and clinical pharmacology data — ideally including ADCC and CDC bioassays alongside PK — are required to support the biosimilarity claim.
The bottom line is that the era of glycoengineered antibodies has, in a sense, raised the activation barrier for biosimilar entry.
Companies that previously could field rituximab biosimilars within months of patent expiry find that they cannot do the same for obinutuzumab within years.
This is a feature, not a bug, of the engineering — the same molecular precision that makes the drug more potent also makes it harder to replicate.
From a payer perspective, this is a concern: glycoengineered drugs may face less price erosion from biosimilars and stay expensive for longer.
From the originator perspective, it's a strategic advantage.
Either way, the lesson for you as students is that the molecular sophistication of modern antibody drugs is a double-edged sword for accessibility.
Engineering excellence delays competition.
The economics of glycoengineering and the economics of biosimilars are linked in ways that the industry is still working out.
